user.title: Battery System
user.description: Configure your battery. You need a sensor that reports the current state of charge (SOC) as a percentage (0-100%).

data.min_soc_floor_percent: Minimum SOC floor — battery will never discharge below this level

data_description.battery_soc_entity: Select the sensor that reports your battery's current charge level as a percentage. Example: sensor.battery_soc, sensor.powerwall_charge. This is NOT the power sensor (kW) — it must be the charge level (%).
data_description.battery_capacity_kwh: The usable capacity of your battery in kWh. Check your battery specs — for example, a Tesla Powerwall 2 is 13.5 kWh, a BYD HVS is 5.1-12.8 kWh.
data_description.min_soc_floor_percent: The battery will never be discharged below this percentage. Set to 10-20% to preserve battery health and keep a safety reserve. Your inverter may also enforce its own minimum.

export_window.title: Export Bonus Window & Rates
export_window.description: Configure your energy retailer's export bonus window (peak feed-in tariff hours) and your electricity rates. All rates are in your local currency per kWh.

data.export_bonus_rate: Export bonus rate — what you earn per kWh during the bonus window
data.standard_export_rate: Standard export rate — what you earn per kWh outside the bonus window
data.standard_import_rate: Standard import rate — what you pay per kWh to buy from the grid

data_description.export_bonus_start: When your retailer's high feed-in tariff window starts. Example: 18:00 for an evening peak export bonus.
data_description.export_bonus_end: When the high feed-in tariff window ends. Example: 20:00.
data_description.export_bonus_rate: The rate you earn per kWh during the bonus window. Example: 0.45 for 45 cents/kWh. Check your retailer's feed-in tariff schedule.
data_description.standard_export_rate: The rate you earn per kWh for exports outside the bonus window. Example: 0.05 for 5 cents/kWh.
data_description.standard_import_rate: The standard rate you pay to buy electricity from the grid. Example: 0.30 for 30 cents/kWh.

solar_forecast.title: Solar Forecast
solar_forecast.description: Select your solar production forecast sensor. Forecast.Solar and Solcast formats are auto-detected. The optimizer uses this to predict when your panels will generate excess energy.

data.solar_forecast_entity: Solar forecast sensor (energy in kWh or power in W/kW)

data_description.solar_forecast_entity: Select a sensor that provides solar generation forecasts. Supported integrations: Forecast.Solar (sensor.energy_production_today), Solcast (sensor.solcast_pv_forecast_today), or any sensor with hourly forecast data in its attributes. The format is auto-detected.

optional_tariffs.title: Additional Tariff Periods (optional)
optional_tariffs.description: Configure special rate periods. Leave all fields blank if you only have a flat-rate tariff. These help the optimizer decide when to charge from the grid vs discharge the battery.

data.peak_import_rate: Peak import rate — what you pay per kWh during peak hours
data.grid_charging_enabled: Allow grid charging during free/cheap import windows

data_description.free_import_start: Start of your free or discounted import window. Example: 11:00 for a midday solar soak tariff. Leave blank if you don't have one.
data_description.free_import_end: End of the free/cheap import window. Example: 14:00.
data_description.peak_import_start: Start of your peak import rate window (highest cost period). Example: 17:00 for an evening peak.
data_description.peak_import_end: End of the peak rate window. Example: 21:00.
data_description.peak_import_rate: The rate you pay per kWh during peak hours. Example: 0.55 for 55 cents/kWh.
data_description.grid_charging_enabled: If enabled, the optimizer may charge the battery from the grid during free/cheap import windows to maximise savings. Only enable if your tariff allows cost-effective grid charging.

optional_consumption.title: Home Consumption Learning (optional)
optional_consumption.description: Select a sensor that tracks your home's total energy consumption. The optimizer learns your usage patterns over time (weekday vs weekend, time of day) to make better scheduling decisions. Skip this if you prefer a static baseline.

data.consumption_entity: Home consumption sensor (cumulative energy in kWh)
data.consumption_baseline_kw: Baseline consumption — your average home power draw
data.consumption_profile_granularity: Profile granularity — how the optimizer groups usage patterns
data.consumption_lookback_days: How many days of history to learn from

data_description.consumption_entity: Select a sensor that tracks total home energy consumption (cumulative kWh). Example: sensor.home_energy_consumption, sensor.grid_consumption. This must be an ENERGY sensor (kWh), not a power sensor (kW/W). It should increase over time as energy is used.
data_description.consumption_baseline_kw: Your home's average power draw in kW, used as a fallback before the system learns your patterns. Example: 0.8 for a typical home. A home with AC/heating may average 1.5-2.5 kW.
data_description.consumption_profile_granularity: How the optimizer groups your usage patterns. 'Weekday/Weekend' is recommended as most homes use different amounts on work days vs rest days.
data_description.consumption_lookback_days: Number of days of history to analyse when learning your consumption patterns. 30 days is a good default. Use more (90-180) for seasonal patterns.

optional_weather.title: Weather Integration (optional)
optional_weather.description: Adding a weather entity improves forecast accuracy. On cloudy/rainy days, the optimizer will reduce its confidence in solar predictions and adjust the schedule conservatively. It also learns how temperature affects your home's energy use (heating/cooling).

data_description.weather_entity: Select your weather integration entity. Example: weather.home, weather.openweathermap. Any HA weather integration works — it provides cloud cover and temperature data.

optional_battery_rates.title: Inverter Rate Limits (optional)
optional_battery_rates.description: Set the maximum charge, discharge, and export power for your inverter. The defaults (5 kW) work for most residential systems. Only change these if you know your inverter's exact limits.

data.max_charge_rate_kw: Max charge rate — maximum power the inverter can push into the battery
data.max_discharge_rate_kw: Max discharge rate — maximum power the inverter can pull from the battery
data.max_export_limit_kw: Max grid export — maximum power your system can export to the grid
data.max_export_limit_entity: Dynamic export limit sensor (optional — overrides the static value above)

data_description.max_charge_rate_kw: Maximum charging power in kW. This is a POWER value (kW), not energy (kWh). Example: 5.0 for a 5 kW hybrid inverter. Check your inverter's datasheet.
data_description.max_discharge_rate_kw: Maximum discharging power in kW. Usually the same as charge rate. Example: 5.0.
data_description.max_export_limit_kw: Maximum power your system can export to the grid in kW. This may be limited by your retailer or network operator. Example: 5.0 for a standard 5 kW export limit, or 10.0 if approved for higher export.
data_description.max_export_limit_entity: Optional: select a sensor that provides a dynamic export limit (in kW). Useful if your network operator adjusts your export limit via DRED or similar. Example: sensor.export_limit. Leave empty to use the static value above.

error.entity_not_found: The selected entity was not found. Make sure it exists and is available.

data.aggressiveness: Aggressiveness — 0 = hold charge conservatively, 1 = maximize export revenue
data.lookahead_hours: Optimization lookahead — how far ahead to plan
data.recalculation_interval_minutes: Recalculation interval — how often to re-optimize
data.bridge_to_fallback_time: Fallback bridge-to time (if no solar forecast data)

data_description.aggressiveness: Controls the trade-off between saving energy for home use vs exporting for revenue. 0.3 is a good starting point. Increase towards 1.0 if you want maximum export income.
data_description.slot_granularity_minutes: The time resolution of the schedule. 30 minutes is recommended for a good balance of accuracy and performance.
data_description.lookahead_hours: How many hours ahead the optimizer plans. 24-48 hours covers a full day-night cycle. Longer lookahead is useful with multi-day forecast data.
data_description.recalculation_interval_minutes: How often the schedule is recalculated. 30 minutes balances responsiveness with system load.
data_description.bridge_to_fallback_time: If solar forecast is unavailable, the optimizer ensures enough battery charge to bridge to this time. Example: 06:00 (next sunrise).

data.grid_charging_enabled: Allow grid charging during cheap windows

data_description.battery_soc_entity: Sensor reporting battery charge level as percentage (0-100%). NOT power in kW.
data_description.solar_forecast_entity: Solar forecast sensor with hourly predictions in attributes.
data_description.consumption_entity: Cumulative energy sensor in kWh (increases over time). NOT instantaneous power in kW/W.
data_description.weather_entity: Weather forecast entity providing cloud cover and temperature.
data_description.max_export_limit_entity: Optional sensor providing dynamic export limit in kW.

data.fallback_mode: Fallback mode when data is unavailable

data_description.fallback_mode: What to do when the optimizer can't run (missing data, errors). 'Conservative hold' keeps the battery at current level. 'Last known good' replays the previous schedule.
data_description.solver_timeout_seconds: Maximum time allowed for the LP solver. Increase if you see timeout errors on slower hardware.
data_description.data_retention_days: How long to keep historical consumption data for learning. Longer retention captures seasonal patterns.
data_description.min_soc_floor_percent: Battery will never discharge below this percentage. Acts as a safety reserve.
